84	
85	## Overview
86	
87	DDx is a terminal-first developer platform. Its visual language is **sober, utilitarian, and purposely non-decorative** — the antithesis of AI-gloss, dreamy illustration, and gradient excess. Every visual choice serves comprehension, not style.
88	
89	The product's three-color identity maps directly to its core metaphor: the **lever, the load, and the fulcrum**. Abstraction is the lever — it transmits intent across levels of the stack. LLMs are the load — probabilistic, costly, not fully in control. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum — the stable pivot where judgment meets execution. These are not decorative categories; they are architectural facts about how DDx works, and the palette encodes them.
90	
91	The primary surface is documentation — markdown, terminal output, CLI reference pages. Visuals live alongside text, not in competition with it. The register is developer-grade: precise, legible at a glance, trustworthy at scale.
92	
93	## Colors
94	
95	Three brand colors anchor the palette, each carrying semantic weight from the metaphor system.
96	
97	- **Primary (#4878C6 — lever blue):** The mechanism. Used for primary actions, links, dispatch signals, and any UI element that transmits intent — the lever arm in motion.
98	- **Secondary (#35A35F — fulcrum green):** The pivot. Used for success states, progress indicators, and human-AI collaboration surfaces — stable ground where control is exercised.
99	- **Tertiary (#8E35A3 — load purple):** The weight. Used for AI/agent surfaces, stochastic outputs, cost indicators, and any context where probabilistic or uncertain mass is present.
100	
101	These three colors appear as radial gradients on feature cards on the landing page (15% opacity on white), preserving legibility while anchoring semantic meaning. At full opacity they are used only for icons, badges, and deliberate accent marks — never as large fill blocks.
102	
103	Surface and text tokens support both light-mode documentation and dark terminal contexts. The terminal palette (`terminal-bg: #0F1117`, `terminal-fg: #E8EAF0`) matches the tone of the animated demo recordings that are the product's primary visual artifact.
104	
105	## Typography
106	
107	Two families. One for prose and UI; one for code.
108	
109	- **Inter** handles all headings, body copy, labels, and navigation. It is neutral without being generic: the right weight for technical documentation that needs to be read fast, not admired.
110	- **JetBrains Mono** handles all code blocks, terminal output, CLI references, and inline code. Monospace is not decorative here — it is the medium. Most of what DDx produces and consumes is text in a terminal.
111	
112	Type scale is conservative: three heading sizes, two body sizes, one label size. No display sizes. This is reference material, not marketing copy.
113	
114	## Layout
115	
116	Wide-page layout, no max-width on docs pages. Content takes the space it needs; sidebars carry navigation, not decoration. Spacing scale is small-grained at the low end (4px grid) to support tight CLI output tables and artifact metadata blocks.
117	
118	Horizontal rhythm: content rarely bleeds to the viewport edge. Comfortable line lengths (65–80 characters) for prose sections; full width for tables and code blocks.
119	
120	## Components
121	
122	**button-primary:** The primary call-to-action — lever blue with white text. Used for "Get Started" and primary agent-dispatch triggers. Square-ish radius (8px), not pill. Pill buttons signal consumer software; DDx is a developer tool.
123	
124	**button-alt:** Secondary action — light surface with dark text. Used alongside button-primary for "Learn More" and non-destructive secondary choices.
125	
126	**feature-card:** White card with 12px radius, 24px interior padding. Background accent is a radial gradient at 15% opacity using the appropriate brand color (blue for lever/tracker, green for fulcrum/plugins, purple for load/execution). Never a full-opacity color fill.
127	
128	**terminal-block:** Dark (#0F1117) block with light text (#E8EAF0), 8px radius, 16px padding. Used for CLI command examples, agent output, and demo code. This is the product's native context; treat it as first-class, not supplementary.
129	
130	## Do's and Don'ts
131	
132	**Do:**
133	- Use the three brand colors with semantic intent — blue for action/dispatch, green for collaboration/success, purple for AI/stochastic contexts.
134	- Maintain the sober, utilitarian register. If an image could appear on an AI company's landing page, it does not belong here.
135	- Render the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor concretely: physical objects, mechanical diagrams, DAG graphs, state machines. Abstract geometry is acceptable only if the metaphor parses without explanatory copy.
136	- Use dark backgrounds for terminal-context visuals. The animated demo recordings set the tone; generated images should match it.
137	- Keep file sizes under 200KB for generated visuals. Compress and downsize before committing.
138	
139	**Don't:**
140	- Use gradient washes, glows, lens flares, particle effects, or any visual treatment that signals "AI art." This is a developer tool, not a product launch deck.
141	- Use the three brand colors interchangeably. Each color carries a specific role in the metaphor system; mixing them dilutes the signal.
142	- Place the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor as decorative background texture. If it appears, it carries meaning.
143	- Use illustration styles that anthropomorphize the AI components (no robots, no glowing brains, no circuit-board heads). LLMs are load — treat them as mass, not character.
144	- Ship visuals that require explanatory copy to parse. If the metaphor doesn't read in the image, revise the visual concept, not the caption.
145	
146	## Metaphor System
147	
148	The lever/load/fulcrum triad is the load-bearing metaphor for all visual communication across the three lenses (principles, tools, user workflow).
149	
150	| Element | Role | Visual treatment |
151	|---|---|---|
152	| Lever | Abstraction transmitting intent across levels | Arm, mechanism, directional force, stack layers |
153	| Load | LLM output — probabilistic, costly, non-uniform | Mass, weight, irregular shape, probabilistic scatter |
154	| Fulcrum | Human-AI collaboration — the stable pivot | Contact point, pivot, seam between human judgment and agent execution |
155	
156	The three brand colors map to these roles. Visuals for the **principles lens** use this metaphor directly and concretely — not as abstract icon, but as mechanical or physical object. Visuals for the **tools lens** encode the metaphor through product structure (DAGs, worktrees, plugin composition). The **workflow capstone** shows the full loop: lever in motion, load being moved, fulcrum holding.
157	
158	A visual that replaces the metaphor with generic tech imagery (nodes, code text, glowing interfaces) fails the brand-fit criterion regardless of color accuracy.
159

158	
159	## Overview
160	
161	DDx is a platform for document-driven AI development. The visual identity reflects its
162	guiding metaphor: **mechanical advantage** — levers, loads, and fulcrums. Nothing decorative.
163	Everything structural.
164	
165	The design system spans two distinct surfaces that share one token vocabulary:
166	
167	- **Admin Console** — a high-density terminal dashboard. Maximum information per pixel.
168	  Think a hardware diagnostic panel or a printed maintenance ledger. Inter-only typography,
169	  0px border-radius everywhere, zero shadows, 4px spacing grid.
170	
171	- **Marketing Microsite** — a scholarly engineering monograph. Generous whitespace, 128px
172	  section breaks, editorial Newsreader serif body copy, Space Grotesk mono-labels. The same
173	  palette applied at low density.
174	
175	Both surfaces reject: gradients, glassmorphism, rounded-pill buttons, drop shadows,
176	decorative icons, AI-gloss aesthetics. Hierarchy comes from tonal layering and hairline
177	borders alone.
178	
179	## Colors
180	
181	The palette is three accents over a warm-paper foundation. All surfaces are warm-toned
182	(cream, bone, near-black) rather than cold gray or pure white/black.
183	
184	**Accent triple** — every interactive or state-bearing element maps to one of three roles:
185	
186	- **Lever** (`#3B5B7A` light / `#7BA3CC` dark) — the force multiplier. Primary buttons,
187	  active nav indicators, links, focus rings. Cold Steel Blue.
188	- **Load** (`#A8801F` light / `#D4A53D` dark) — the thing being moved. In-progress states,
189	  claimed beads, emphasis text, warnings. Aged Brass / Warm Brass.
190	- **Fulcrum** (`#3F4147` light / `#9CA0A8` dark) — the pivot point. Neutral structural
191	  accents, secondary actions, iron-gray elements. Iron Gray / Neutral Silver.
192	
193	**Surfaces** — three tiers of warmth:
194	
195	- Canvas (`#F4EFE6` / `#1A1815`) — page background. Never used for card fill.
196	- Surface (`#FBF8F2` / `#26231F`) — the default container background.
197	- Elevated (`#FFFFFF` / `#2E2A25`) — inputs, modal overlays, highest-layer panels.
198	
199	**Text** — ink and muted sepia, never cold gray:
200	
201	- Ink (`#1F2125` / `#EDE6D6`) — primary content.
202	- Muted (`#6B6558` / `#8E8674`) — secondary labels, metadata, timestamps.
203	
204	**Borders** — one value per mode: `#E4DDD0` (light hairline tan) / `#34302A` (dark ember).
205	
206	Do not use standard Tailwind gray-XXX classes. All colors must come from this token set.
207	
208	## Typography
209	
210	Two typographic registers:
211	
212	**Admin (dense):** Inter only, all weights. Scale is compressed — headlines stay small to
213	maximize screen real estate. Hierarchy through weight (400→800) and case (sentence vs ALL
214	CAPS). Monospace is `ui-monospace` / JetBrains Mono for IDs, timestamps, and system data.
215	
216	**Microsite (editorial):** Inter for all structural/UI text. Newsreader serif for body
217	prose (the academic signature of the site). Space Grotesk for mono-labels and CTAs — more
218	legible than full monospace, less cold.
219	
220	Typography rules that apply everywhere:
221	- `label-caps`: always uppercase, always 0.05em letter-spacing. Never sentence case.
222	- `mono-code`: reserved for machine-readable data — IDs, hashes, timestamps, CLI output.
223	- Headlines use negative letter-spacing (`-0.01em` to `-0.02em`). No loose tracking on
224	  large type.
225	- Body text never exceeds 720px / ~70ch column width.
226	
227	## Layout
228	
229	**Admin layout shell:**
230	- 256px fixed left sidebar (nav + branding)
231	- 40px fixed top bar (search + status + tools)
232	- Scrollable main area with 12px gutter padding
233	- 4px base unit. All spacing in multiples of 4.
234	- 12-column grid for main content areas
235	- Sections separated by border lines, not whitespace
236	
237	**Microsite layout:**
238	- 1200px max content container, centered
239	- 720px prose column (h1 + body paragraphs)
240	- 128px (`xl: section`) vertical rhythm between major sections
241	- Fixed 64px top nav
242	
243	**Both:** No horizontal scrolling. No breakpoint below desktop in the admin. Microsite
244	responds at md (768px) for nav collapse.
245	
246	## Elevation & Depth
247	
248	Zero shadows anywhere. Depth is communicated exclusively through:
249	
250	1. **Tonal layering** — Canvas → Surface → Elevated is the progression. In dark mode
251	   this inverts relative lightness but the same three tiers apply.
252	2. **Hairline borders** — 1px `border-line` separates every functional zone.
253	3. **Left-rail accent** — The active nav item gets a 2px left border in `accent-lever`.
254	   This is the strongest spatial indicator in the admin UI.
255	4. **Inversion for primary actions** — `accent-lever` fill with `bg-elevated` text. The
256	   high-contrast inversion reads as "press me" without any shadow.
257	
258	Focus rings use `accent-lever` border color (no outer glow/box-shadow).
259	
260	## Shapes
261	
262	**Admin console:** 0px border-radius on everything — buttons, inputs, cards, badges,
263	table containers. Completely sharp. Exceptions: `border-radius: 9999px` is allowed for
264	status indicator dots only (the 2×2px pulse indicators).
265	
266	**Microsite:** 4px default radius (`rounded-DEFAULT`) for interactive elements (buttons,
267	inputs). Still reads as nearly sharp — just enough to distinguish interactive from static.
268	Cards remain fully sharp (0px).
269	
270	## Components
271	
272	**Buttons**
273	- Primary: `accent-lever` fill, `bg-elevated` text, 0px radius, `label-caps` typography,
274	  uppercase, tracked. Hover: opacity 90%. No shadow.
275	- Ghost: transparent fill, `border-line` border, same shape. Hover: `bg-surface` fill.
276	- Destructive: `error` fill, white text.
277	
278	**Status Badges**
279	Sharp rectangles (`border-radius: 0px`). Color = semantic role tint at 10% opacity for
280	background, full color for text and border at 20% opacity. Typography is `label-caps`.
281	States: OPEN/RUNNING (steel), IN-PROGRESS/CLAIMED (brass), CLOSED/COMPLETED (green),
282	BLOCKED/FAULT (red), PARKED/IDLE (muted).
283	
284	**Data Tables**
285	Full-width, `border-collapse`. Header row: subtle canvas tint. Row dividers: `border-line`
286	1px. Row hover: light canvas tint. ID columns: `mono-code`. Actions column: icon-only.
287	Progress bars: 1px height, no radius, `accent-load` fill.
288	
289	**Navigation (Admin)**
290	Left sidebar, fixed 256px. Active item: 2px `accent-lever` left border + bold text +

293	
294	**Navigation (Microsite)**
295	Fixed 64px top bar. Logo: `font-mono font-bold tracking-tighter`. Links: 12px uppercase

158	
159	## Overview
160	
161	DDx is a platform for document-driven AI development. The visual identity reflects its
162	guiding metaphor: **mechanical advantage** — levers, loads, and fulcrums. Nothing decorative.
163	Everything structural.
164	
165	The design system spans two distinct surfaces that share one token vocabulary:
166	
167	- **Admin Console** — a high-density terminal dashboard. Maximum information per pixel.
168	  Think a hardware diagnostic panel or a printed maintenance ledger. Inter-only typography,
169	  0px border-radius everywhere, zero shadows, 4px spacing grid.
170	
171	- **Marketing Microsite** — a scholarly engineering monograph. Generous whitespace, 128px
172	  section breaks, editorial Newsreader serif body copy, Space Grotesk mono-labels. The same
173	  palette applied at low density.
174	
175	Both surfaces reject: gradients, glassmorphism, rounded-pill buttons, drop shadows,
176	decorative icons, AI-gloss aesthetics. Hierarchy comes from tonal layering and hairline
177	borders alone.
178	
179	## Colors
180	
181	The palette is three accents over a warm-paper foundation. All surfaces are warm-toned
182	(cream, bone, near-black) rather than cold gray or pure white/black.
183	
184	**Accent triple** — every interactive or state-bearing element maps to one of three roles:
185	
186	- **Lever** (`#3B5B7A` light / `#7BA3CC` dark) — the force multiplier. Primary buttons,
187	  active nav indicators, links, focus rings. Cold Steel Blue.
188	- **Load** (`#A8801F` light / `#D4A53D` dark) — the thing being moved. In-progress states,
189	  claimed beads, emphasis text, warnings. Aged Brass / Warm Brass.
190	- **Fulcrum** (`#3F4147` light / `#9CA0A8` dark) — the pivot point. Neutral structural
191	  accents, secondary actions, iron-gray elements. Iron Gray / Neutral Silver.
192	
193	**Surfaces** — three tiers of warmth:
194	
195	- Canvas (`#F4EFE6` / `#1A1815`) — page background. Never used for card fill.
196	- Surface (`#FBF8F2` / `#26231F`) — the default container background.
197	- Elevated (`#FFFFFF` / `#2E2A25`) — inputs, modal overlays, highest-layer panels.
198	
199	**Text** — ink and muted sepia, never cold gray:
200	
201	- Ink (`#1F2125` / `#EDE6D6`) — primary content.
202	- Muted (`#6B6558` / `#8E8674`) — secondary labels, metadata, timestamps.
203	
204	**Borders** — one value per mode: `#E4DDD0` (light hairline tan) / `#34302A` (dark ember).
205	
206	Do not use standard Tailwind gray-XXX classes. All colors must come from this token set.
207	
208	## Typography
209	
210	Two typographic registers:
211	
212	**Admin (dense):** Inter only, all weights. Scale is compressed — headlines stay small to
213	maximize screen real estate. Hierarchy through weight (400→800) and case (sentence vs ALL
214	CAPS). Monospace is `ui-monospace` / JetBrains Mono for IDs, timestamps, and system data.
215	
216	**Microsite (editorial):** Inter for all structural/UI text. Newsreader serif for body
217	prose (the academic signature of the site). Space Grotesk for mono-labels and CTAs — more
218	legible than full monospace, less cold.
219	
220	Typography rules that apply everywhere:
221	- `label-caps`: always uppercase, always 0.05em letter-spacing. Never sentence case.
222	- `mono-code`: reserved for machine-readable data — IDs, hashes, timestamps, CLI output.
223	- Headlines use negative letter-spacing (`-0.01em` to `-0.02em`). No loose tracking on
224	  large type.
225	- Body text never exceeds 720px / ~70ch column width.
226	
227	## Layout
228	
229	**Admin layout shell:**
230	- 256px fixed left sidebar (nav + branding)
231	- 40px fixed top bar (search + status + tools)
232	- Scrollable main area with 12px gutter padding
233	- 4px base unit. All spacing in multiples of 4.
234	- 12-column grid for main content areas
235	- Sections separated by border lines, not whitespace
236	
237	**Microsite layout:**
238	- 1200px max content container, centered
239	- 720px prose column (h1 + body paragraphs)
240	- 128px (`xl: section`) vertical rhythm between major sections
241	- Fixed 64px top nav
242	
243	**Both:** No horizontal scrolling. No breakpoint below desktop in the admin. Microsite
244	responds at md (768px) for nav collapse.
245	
246	## Elevation & Depth
247	
248	Zero shadows anywhere. Depth is communicated exclusively through:
249	
250	1. **Tonal layering** — Canvas → Surface → Elevated is the progression. In dark mode
251	   this inverts relative lightness but the same three tiers apply.
252	2. **Hairline borders** — 1px `border-line` separates every functional zone.
253	3. **Left-rail accent** — The active nav item gets a 2px left border in `accent-lever`.
254	   This is the strongest spatial indicator in the admin UI.
255	4. **Inversion for primary actions** — `accent-lever` fill with `bg-elevated` text. The
256	   high-contrast inversion reads as "press me" without any shadow.
257	
258	Focus rings use `accent-lever` border color (no outer glow/box-shadow).
259	
260	## Shapes
261	
262	**Admin console:** 0px border-radius on everything — buttons, inputs, cards, badges,
263	table containers. Completely sharp. Exceptions: `border-radius: 9999px` is allowed for
264	status indicator dots only (the 2×2px pulse indicators).
265	
266	**Microsite:** 4px default radius (`rounded-DEFAULT`) for interactive elements (buttons,
267	inputs). Still reads as nearly sharp — just enough to distinguish interactive from static.
268	Cards remain fully sharp (0px).
269	
270	## Components
271	
272	**Buttons**
273	- Primary: `accent-lever` fill, `bg-elevated` text, 0px radius, `label-caps` typography,
274	  uppercase, tracked. Hover: opacity 90%. No shadow.
275	- Ghost: transparent fill, `border-line` border, same shape. Hover: `bg-surface` fill.
276	- Destructive: `error` fill, white text.
277	
278	**Status Badges**
279	Sharp rectangles (`border-radius: 0px`). Color = semantic role tint at 10% opacity for
280	background, full color for text and border at 20% opacity. Typography is `label-caps`.
281	States: OPEN/RUNNING (steel), IN-PROGRESS/CLAIMED (brass), CLOSED/COMPLETED (green),
282	BLOCKED/FAULT (red), PARKED/IDLE (muted).
283	
284	**Data Tables**
285	Full-width, `border-collapse`. Header row: subtle canvas tint. Row dividers: `border-line`
286	1px. Row hover: light canvas tint. ID columns: `mono-code`. Actions column: icon-only.
287	Progress bars: 1px height, no radius, `accent-load` fill.
288	
289	**Navigation (Admin)**
290	Left sidebar, fixed 256px. Active item: 2px `accent-lever` left border + bold text + 
291	`bg-surface` background. Inactive: transparent left border, `fg-muted` text. Hover:
292	`bg-surface` background.
293	
294	**Navigation (Microsite)**
295	Fixed 64px top bar. Logo: `font-mono font-bold tracking-tighter`. Links: 12px uppercase
296	Space Grotesk, 8-unit spacing. Active: underline. Mobile: collapses at 768px.
297	
298	**Inputs / Search**
299	`bg-elevated` background, `border-line` border, 0px radius (admin) / 4px (microsite).
300	Focus: border becomes `accent-lever`. No outline ring. Placeholder: `fg-muted`.
301	
302	**Sidebar Nav (Admin)**
303	Items: 12px vertical padding, 24px horizontal. Leading icon (18px). All-caps label.
304	Bottom section: Docs, Logs — separated by a `border-line` top border.
305	
306	**Terminal Pane**
307	Background: `terminal-bg-deep` (`#0F0E0C`), foreground: `terminal-fg` (`#D8D2C4`).
308	Font: mono-code stack. 4px border-radius on the outer container.
309	
310	**Editorial Cards (Microsite Principles Grid)**
311	Square `aspect-square`, 48px padding, white at 50% opacity over canvas. `border-line`
312	hairline. Hover: border color shifts to thematic accent. Interior animated underline:
313	`h-[2px] w-8 → w-full` transition.
314	
315	**Pull Quote (Microsite)**
316	2px `accent-load` left rail. 30% white background. Italic `body-editorial` text. No icon.

84	
85	## Overview
86	
87	DDx is a terminal-first developer platform. Its visual language is **sober, utilitarian, and purposely non-decorative** — the antithesis of AI-gloss, dreamy illustration, and gradient excess. Every visual choice serves comprehension, not style.
88	
89	The product's three-color identity maps directly to its core metaphor: the **lever, the load, and the fulcrum**. Abstraction is the lever — it transmits intent across levels of the stack. LLMs are the load — probabilistic, costly, not fully in control. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum — the stable pivot where judgment meets execution. These are not decorative categories; they are architectural facts about how DDx works, and the palette encodes them.
90	
91	The primary surface is documentation — markdown, terminal output, CLI reference pages. Visuals live alongside text, not in competition with it. The register is developer-grade: precise, legible at a glance, trustworthy at scale.
92	
93	## Colors
94	
95	Three brand colors anchor the palette, each carrying semantic weight from the metaphor system.
96	
97	- **Primary (#4878C6 — lever blue):** The mechanism. Used for primary actions, links, dispatch signals, and any UI element that transmits intent — the lever arm in motion.
98	- **Secondary (#35A35F — fulcrum green):** The pivot. Used for success states, progress indicators, and human-AI collaboration surfaces — stable ground where control is exercised.
99	- **Tertiary (#8E35A3 — load purple):** The weight. Used for AI/agent surfaces, stochastic outputs, cost indicators, and any context where probabilistic or uncertain mass is present.
100	
101	These three colors appear as radial gradients on feature cards on the landing page (15% opacity on white), preserving legibility while anchoring semantic meaning. At full opacity they are used only for icons, badges, and deliberate accent marks — never as large fill blocks.
102	
103	Surface and text tokens support both light-mode documentation and dark terminal contexts. The terminal palette (`terminal-bg: #0F1117`, `terminal-fg: #E8EAF0`) matches the tone of the animated demo recordings that are the product's primary visual artifact.
104	
105	## Typography
106	
107	Two families. One for prose and UI; one for code.
108	
109	- **Inter** handles all headings, body copy, labels, and navigation. It is neutral without being generic: the right weight for technical documentation that needs to be read fast, not admired.
110	- **JetBrains Mono** handles all code blocks, terminal output, CLI references, and inline code. Monospace is not decorative here — it is the medium. Most of what DDx produces and consumes is text in a terminal.
111	
112	Type scale is conservative: three heading sizes, two body sizes, one label size. No display sizes. This is reference material, not marketing copy.
113	
114	## Layout
115	
116	Wide-page layout, no max-width on docs pages. Content takes the space it needs; sidebars carry navigation, not decoration. Spacing scale is small-grained at the low end (4px grid) to support tight CLI output tables and artifact metadata blocks.
117	
118	Horizontal rhythm: content rarely bleeds to the viewport edge. Comfortable line lengths (65–80 characters) for prose sections; full width for tables and code blocks.
119	
120	## Components
121	
122	**button-primary:** The primary call-to-action — lever blue with white text. Used for "Get Started" and primary agent-dispatch triggers. Square-ish radius (8px), not pill. Pill buttons signal consumer software; DDx is a developer tool.
123	
124	**button-alt:** Secondary action — light surface with dark text. Used alongside button-primary for "Learn More" and non-destructive secondary choices.
125	
126	**feature-card:** White card with 12px radius, 24px interior padding. Background accent is a radial gradient at 15% opacity using the appropriate brand color (blue for lever/tracker, green for fulcrum/plugins, purple for load/execution). Never a full-opacity color fill.
127	
128	**terminal-block:** Dark (#0F1117) block with light text (#E8EAF0), 8px radius, 16px padding. Used for CLI command examples, agent output, and demo code. This is the product's native context; treat it as first-class, not supplementary.
129	
130	## Do's and Don'ts
131	
132	**Do:**
133	- Use the three brand colors with semantic intent — blue for action/dispatch, green for collaboration/success, purple for AI/stochastic contexts.
134	- Maintain the sober, utilitarian register. If an image could appear on an AI company's landing page, it does not belong here.
135	- Render the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor concretely: physical objects, mechanical diagrams, DAG graphs, state machines. Abstract geometry is acceptable only if the metaphor parses without explanatory copy.
136	- Use dark backgrounds for terminal-context visuals. The animated demo recordings set the tone; generated images should match it.
137	- Keep file sizes under 200KB for generated visuals. Compress and downsize before committing.
138	
139	**Don't:**
140	- Use gradient washes, glows, lens flares, particle effects, or any visual treatment that signals "AI art." This is a developer tool, not a product launch deck.
141	- Use the three brand colors interchangeably. Each color carries a specific role in the metaphor system; mixing them dilutes the signal.
142	- Place the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor as decorative background texture. If it appears, it carries meaning.
143	- Use illustration styles that anthropomorphize the AI components (no robots, no glowing brains, no circuit-board heads). LLMs are load — treat them as mass, not character.
144	- Ship visuals that require explanatory copy to parse. If the metaphor doesn't read in the image, revise the visual concept, not the caption.
145	
146	## Metaphor System
147	
148	The lever/load/fulcrum triad is the load-bearing metaphor for all visual communication across the three lenses (principles, tools, user workflow).
149	
150	| Element | Role | Visual treatment |
151	|---|---|---|
152	| Lever | Abstraction transmitting intent across levels | Arm, mechanism, directional force, stack layers |
153	| Load | LLM output — probabilistic, costly, non-uniform | Mass, weight, irregular shape, probabilistic scatter |
154	| Fulcrum | Human-AI collaboration — the stable pivot | Contact point, pivot, seam between human judgment and agent execution |
155	
156	The three brand colors map to these roles. Visuals for the **principles lens** use this metaphor directly and concretely — not as abstract icon, but as mechanical or physical object. Visuals for the **tools lens** encode the metaphor through product structure (DAGs, worktrees, plugin composition). The **workflow capstone** shows the full loop: lever in motion, load being moved, fulcrum holding.
157	
158	A visual that replaces the metaphor with generic tech imagery (nodes, code text, glowing interfaces) fails the brand-fit criterion regardless of color accuracy.
159
